cards.definition: A decision problem is a computational problem in which each input instance is mapped to a binary outcome, typically "yes" or "no." Formally, it can be modeled as a function from strings over an alphabet to {0,1}, forming the basis for complexity theory and computability analysis.

A language is a formally defined set of strings constructed over a finite alphabet. In theoretical computer science, languages correspond to decision problems, where membership represents a "yes" instance and non-membership represents a "no" instance.

An alphabet is a finite, non-empty set of discrete symbols used as the foundational building blocks for constructing strings. These symbols are atomic and indivisible, defining the domain over which formal languages and computational systems operate.

A string is a finite sequence of symbols drawn from a given alphabet. Strings serve as the fundamental objects of study in formal language theory and computation, representing inputs to automata, algorithms, and Turing machines.

The Kleene star of a language is the set of all finite-length strings formed by concatenating zero or more strings from that language, including the empty string. It is a key closure operation in formal language theory and regular expressions.

A deterministic finite automaton (DFA) is a computational model consisting of a finite set of states, a transition function, a start state, and accept states. For every state and input symbol, exactly one transition exists, ensuring deterministic computation and predictable behavior.

A nondeterministic finite automaton (NFA) is a computational model where multiple or zero transitions may exist for a given state and input symbol. Computation proceeds along all possible paths, and acceptance occurs if at least one path reaches an accepting state.

An ε-transition is a transition that allows an automaton to move between states without consuming any input symbol. It introduces nondeterminism and simplifies the construction of automata equivalent to regular expressions.

A regular language is a class of formal languages that can be recognized by finite automata or described using regular expressions. These languages are closed under operations such as union, concatenation, and Kleene star.

Intersection is an operation on two languages that produces a new language consisting of strings present in both original languages. It is a key closure property that preserves regularity in formal language theory.

A Turing machine is an abstract computational model with an infinite tape, a read/write head, and a finite set of states. It formalizes the notion of computation and can simulate any algorithmic process.

A universal Turing machine is capable of simulating any other Turing machine given its encoding and input. It represents the theoretical foundation of programmable computers and general-purpose computation.
The tape is an infinite sequence of cells that store symbols from an alphabet. It functions as both input and memory, allowing a Turing machine to perform computations without fixed memory limits.
The head of a Turing machine reads symbols from the tape, writes new symbols, and moves left or right. It enables interaction with memory and executes transitions during computation.
A configuration represents the complete state of a Turing machine at a given step, including its current state, tape contents, and head position. It captures the entire computational snapshot.

The Church-Turing Thesis asserts that any function that can be computed algorithmically can be computed by a Turing machine. It is a foundational assumption in computability theory, though not formally provable.
A decidable language is one for which there exists a Turing machine that halts on all inputs and correctly determines membership. These correspond to problems that are fully algorithmically solvable.
A recognizable language is one where a Turing machine accepts all strings in the language but may loop indefinitely on non-members, reflecting partial computability.
The Halting Problem asks whether a Turing machine halts on a given input. It is undecidable, demonstrating fundamental limits of algorithmic computation.
The acceptance problem asks whether a Turing machine accepts a given input string. It is undecidable and frequently used in reductions to prove other problems undecidable.
Rice’s Theorem states that all non-trivial semantic properties of languages recognized by Turing machines are undecidable, highlighting limits in analyzing program behavior.
P is the class of decision problems solvable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine. It represents efficiently solvable problems in computational complexity.
NP is the class of decision problems for which solutions can be verified in polynomial time. It includes many important problems whose efficient solvability is unknown.
Polynomial time refers to computational complexity bounded by a polynomial in input size, typically O(n^k). It is used as a standard for efficient computation.
A verifier is an algorithm that checks whether a given certificate proves that an input is a valid solution. It operates efficiently, typically in polynomial time.
A certificate is auxiliary information that allows a verifier to efficiently confirm correctness of a solution, playing a key role in NP definitions.
NP-complete problems are the hardest problems in NP, meaning every NP problem can be reduced to them in polynomial time. They are central to the P vs NP question.
SAT asks whether a Boolean formula has a satisfying assignment of truth values. It was the first problem proven NP-complete and is foundational in computational complexity.
3SAT is a restricted version of SAT where each clause has exactly three literals. Despite this constraint, it remains NP-complete and is widely used in reductions.
Vertex Cover asks for the smallest subset of vertices such that every edge in a graph is incident to at least one selected vertex. It is a classic NP-complete problem.
The clique problem asks whether a graph contains a fully connected subset of vertices of a given size. It is NP-complete and central to graph theory.
Subset Sum asks whether a subset of given integers sums to a specified target value. It is NP-complete and widely studied in combinatorial optimization.
An approximation algorithm is a polynomial-time algorithm designed to produce near-optimal solutions to optimization problems where exact solutions are computationally infeasible.
The approximation ratio is the worst-case ratio between the value of the solution produced by an algorithm and the optimal solution, measuring algorithm performance.
A Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (FPTAS) produces solutions within a factor of (1+ε) of optimal, with runtime polynomial in both input size and 1/ε.
A flow network is a directed graph where each edge has a capacity representing the maximum flow allowed. It models systems such as transportation and communication networks.
Capacity is the maximum amount of flow that can pass through an edge in a network, constraining feasible flow assignments in optimization problems.
Flow is the quantity of material or data transmitted through edges of a network, subject to capacity constraints and conservation laws at intermediate nodes.
An augmenting path is a path from source to sink along which additional flow can be pushed in a residual graph, forming the basis of max-flow algorithms.
The Max Flow Min Cut Theorem states that the maximum flow from source to sink equals the capacity of the minimum cut separating them, establishing a key duality in network optimization.
